items.title: Chinese FM urges closer China-ASEAN cooperation to tackle challenges
items.description: Chinese Foreign Minister Wang Yi on Wednesday highlighted the tangible achievements of the China-ASEAN Comprehensive Strategic Partnership over the past five years and stressed the need for greater unity between China and ASEAN in addressing emerging challenges and uncertainties. Wang, also a member of the Political Bureau of the Communist Party of China Central Committee, made the remarks while attending the China-ASEAN Foreign Ministers' Meeting in Manila. He outlined four major areas of progress that have strengthened ties between China and ASEAN. The first is the steady progress in building a community with a shared future with neighboring countries. Wang said China has reached important consensus with eight ASEAN member states on jointly forming a community with a shared future bilaterally. Second, economic and trade cooperation has continued to grow. Following the formal signing of the China-ASEAN Free Trade Area 3.0 Upgrade Protocol, China and ASEAN have remained each other's largest trading partners for six consecutive years, with bilateral trade surpassing $1 trillion in 2025, Wang said. Third, China and ASEAN have demonstrated solidarity in times of crisis. Wang cited China's rapid emergency assistance following major earthquakes in Myanmar and Mindanao in the Philippines as an example of mutual support. Fourth, people-to-people exchanges have expanded significantly. Wang noted that nearly 200 major and well-received events were held during the 2024-2025 ASEAN-China Year of People-to-People Exchanges, while a dedicated visa facilitation scheme for ASEAN countries was introduced to make cross-border travel more convenient. While recognizing the fruitful outcomes of China-ASEAN cooperation, Wang said China is ready to work with ASEAN to ensure that their partnership continues to serve as an engine for Asia's development and revitalization, a model for Asia-Pacific regional cooperation, and a benchmark for the solidarity and self-strengthening of the Global South. Wang put forward four proposals for further advancing China-ASEAN cooperation. First, to safeguard the regional environment for peace and development. Wang said all sides should champion the Asian values of peace, cooperation, openness and inclusiveness, firmly reject unjustified external interference, oppose patching up exclusive small groupings, refuse to be drawn into camp-based confrontation and never allow militarism to stage a comeback. Second, to bolster the resilience of regional development. Wang said that all sides should stay committed to open cooperation, deepen development integration, advance the China-ASEAN Free Trade Area 3.0, and implement the Regional Comprehensive Economic Partnership at a high standard, while enhancing food, energy security and supply chain stability in the region. Third, to advance green and innovation-driven development. Wang said that all sides should expand cooperation in emerging areas such as the digital economy, artificial intelligence, and submarine cables. Efforts should be made to explore synergy with the ASEAN Digital Economy Framework Agreement, and step up collaboration in climate response, clean energy, green agriculture and the blue economy, he added. Fourth, to remain people-centered. Wang said that all sides should promote inclusive and equitable development to ensure that the outcomes of China-ASEAN cooperation are tangible and accessible and deliver greater benefits to the people of all countries. China stands ready to enhance exchanges and cooperation with ASEAN in poverty reduction, public health, education, youth, think tanks, tourism, media, culture and other fields, he noted. Wang also reiterated China's position on the South China Sea issue, urging regional countries to strictly honor the Declaration on the Conduct of Parties in the South China Sea. He called on regional countries to resist external attempts to stoke tensions, reject any nation's efforts to stir trouble in the South China Sea and keep the initiative for safeguarding regional peace and stability firmly in their own hands. Wang said a critical window has opened for finalizing the Code of Conduct (COC) in the South China Sea, calling on all parties to seize the opportunity, prevent external interference and work toward achieving the goal. During the meeting, all parties agreed that China is one of ASEAN's most important neighbors and dialogue partners, as well as its largest trading partner. In the face of a turbulent international landscape, ASEAN countries look forward to closer strategic coordination with China to bolster their own development resilience and advance high-quality connectivity. They also voiced support for deeper cooperation in trade and investment, the digital economy, artificial intelligence, climate change, clean energy and efforts to combat transnational crime. All sides expressed the hope of reaching a Code of Conduct in the South China Sea as early as possible this year, in a joint effort to uphold peace and stability in the region. The meeting adopted the joint statement of the China-ASEAN foreign ministers on addressing the impact of the evolving situation in the Middle East on the region and strengthening regional energy cooperation.

items.title: Cameroun: pourquoi Paul Biya manque-t-il tant aux Camerounais?
items.description: Au moins 45 jours sont passés, et celui dont les services avaient annoncé un court séjour en Europe, n’est toujours pas revenu à la maison. Est-il souffrant? Est-il en vacances pour se requinquer afin de se remettre à la tâche pour le bonheur de ses compatriotes? Les questions, et même les pires hypothèses, animent les débats, officiels ou de rue. A Yaoundé ou à Douala, plus aucun jour ne passe sans que le sujet ne soit au menu. Certes, tout humain tombe malade ou connaît la fatigue physique. Sauf que l’absent tant réclamé par ses concitoyens n’est pas n’importe qui. Il est le président du Cameroun depuis 1982. De 1984 à nos jours, il est régulièrement élu, souvent même sur un seul jour de campagne, où il a de la peine à s’exprimer. Du reste sa dernière élection ne date que d’octobre 2025. Inamovible candidat de son parti, le Rassemblement démocratique du peuple camerounais (RDPC), Paul Biya, puisque c’est de lui qu’il s’agit, peut se targuer du haut de ses 93 piges, d’un palmarès de longévité exceptionnel. Il est, dans le monde, le plus vieux chef d’Etat. En Afrique, seul l’Equato-guinéen, Teodoro Obiang Nguema Mbasogo, le coiffe dans le classement des plus anciens présidents de la république en fonction. Il n’a certainement plus sa jeunesse d’antan, et ne jouit pas non plus d‘une santé inoxydable. Mais, Paul Biya n’en continue pas moins de suivre méticuleusement la vie de sa nation, selon ses lieutenants. Que nenni, rétorque l’opposition qui brandit même l’argument de la vacance du pouvoir après les 45 jours d’absence du chef de l’Etat. Si aucun texte ne statue sur une quelconque durée de l’absence du chef de l’Etat loin du palais d’Etoudi, et surtout du territoire national, il n’en demeure pas moins que le sujet inquiète cette fois-ci. Il fait même rage, mais, comme à l’accoutumée, il ne fait débat qu’au sein de la classe politique. Ce qui est certain, cette absence de seulement-est-on tenté de dire- 45 jours de Paul Biya, ne fait discussion qu’au niveau des politiciens. Le petit peuple lui est habitué à des séjours plus longs du président de la république. A l’époque, les rares apparitions, au Cameroun, de celui qui est affectueusement appelé «Popaul», sont des événements, tant le «chef» se sentait mieux en Suisse. Preuve que le Camerounais lambda est plus préoccupé par la recherche de son «bâton» de manioc quotidien, et davantage prêt à s’enthousiasmer sur les victoires des «Lions Indomptables» en Coupe d’Afrique des Nations ou en Coupe du monde de football, que d’épiloguer sur une présence ou une absence, aussi longue soit-elle, du chef de l’Etat. Cette «absence», qui passe de «court» à «long» séjour, et peut-être à durée indéterminée, du chef de l’Etat prend, plutôt, des airs de positionnement dans une optique, pour ne pas dire une logique, de l’après Biya. En tout cas, l’absence de Paul Biya est loin d’être une tempête dans un verre d’eau, car concernant le sommet de l’Etat. Elle prend même des proportions encore plus importantes, vu que le vice-président dont la nomination est toujours attendue dans la nomenklatura camerounaise, est, pour l’instant, inexistant. Peut-être que, pour des raisons urgentes, car il faut bien un chef, en l’absence du chef, pour diriger. Président Biya, ton peuple t’attend! Par Wakat Séra L’article Cameroun: pourquoi Paul Biya manque-t-il tant aux Camerounais? est apparu en premier sur Wakat Séra .

items.title: Burkina Faso : Sortie ENSOA de 520 jeunes sous-officiers spécialistes renforcent les rangs de l’Armée burkinabè
items.description: ​Le Général de Division Moussa DIALLO, Chef d'Etat-Major Général des Armées a assisté à la cérémonie officielle de sortie de la 14e promotion des élèves Sous-officiers spécialistes de l’Ecole Nationale de Sous-Officiers d'Active-ENSOA , ce mercredi 22 juillet 2026 au Camp Général Bila ZAGRÉ de Kamboincin. Cette promotion est forte de 520 jeunes Sous-officiers, dont 125 personnels féminins. Elle a pour parrain le Lieutenant à la retraite ZOURE Albert et est baptisée « Promotion Sergent-Chef KABORE Tilado », en hommage à son engagement jusqu'au sacrifice suprême. Le délégué de promotion, le Sergent DIBLONI Sansan Augustin a réaffirmé l'engagement de ses pairs à défendre la Patrie partout où le devoir les appelera. ​Pour le Général de Division Célestin SIMPORE, Ministre d’État, Ministre de la Guerre et de la Défense Patriotique, président de la cérémonie, cette cuvée vient renforcer l'effectif des FAN en vue d'en faire l'une des plus performantes de la sous-région. Ce dernier a par ailleurs exhorté les nouveaux Sous-officiers à respecter leur engagement et à faire honneur à leur nom de promotion. Etat-Major Général Des Armées Du Burkina Faso L’article Burkina Faso : Sortie ENSOA de 520 jeunes sous-officiers spécialistes renforcent les rangs de l'Armée burkinabè est apparu en premier sur NetAfrique.net .

items.title: Koudougou : Une mineure de 16 ans sauvée d’un mariage forcé grâce à une mobilisation citoyenne
items.description: La vigilance citoyenne a permis d'éviter le pire. Selon une information rapportée par l’Agence d’Information du Burkina (AIB), une tentative de mariage forcé visant une mineure de 16 ans a été déjouée à Koudougou le 17 juillet 2026. Grâce à l’intervention musclée de la Brigade régionale de protection de l’enfance de Nando, une union arrangée par un tuteur peu scrupuleux a été stoppée. Ce dossier, qui mêle détresse familiale et intérêts commerciaux, remet en lumière la persistance des mariages d'enfants et l'importance cruciale de la dénonciation pour protéger les plus vulnérables. L’affaire, qui a secoué le quartier de la grande mosquée de Koudougou, illustre la fragilité des droits de l'enfant face à certains abus de position d'autorité. Tout commence par une alerte lancée depuis l’étranger par un proche de la famille, inquiet du sort d’une jeune fille de 16 ans. L'enquête menée par la Brigade régionale de protection de l’enfance de Nando révèle un scénario glaçant. Le mis en cause, un commerçant local agissant en qualité d'oncle paternel et de tuteur, avait unilatéralement célébré le mariage de sa nièce à la mosquée le 21 juin dernier. Le motif ? Sceller un partenariat d’affaires avec un fournisseur de boissons chez qui l’adolescente travaillait. Loin de ses cours de couture, la mineure était devenue, malgré elle, le gage d’une alliance commerciale entre deux hommes. Le parcours de la victime est celui d'une résilience mise à rude épreuve. Orpheline de père depuis l'âge de 9 ans suite à un accident en Côte d'Ivoire, elle a été ballottée entre le Mali et le Burkina Faso, souvent à l'insu de sa mère. Informée depuis Abidjan où elle recevait des soins, sa génitrice, Mme Édoua Rolande, a immédiatement opposé un refus catégorique à cette union forcée, sollicitant l'appui des autorités pour permettre à sa fille de poursuivre sa formation professionnelle. Face à la pression des services compétents et du dispositif d’intervention rapide, le tuteur a été contraint de faire marche arrière. Lors de son interpellation, il a officiellement déclaré renoncer à ce projet matrimonial. Pour la jeune victime, ce dénouement est une libération : elle a exprimé sa volonté de se consacrer désormais exclusivement à son apprentissage et à la construction de son avenir. Dans les colonnes de l'Agence d’Information du Burkina (AIB), le commissaire principal de police Mahama Kaboré, chef de la brigade de Nando, a tenu à saluer la collaboration des populations. Il insiste toutefois sur la nécessité d'une vigilance de chaque instant contre les abus d'autorité et les mariages précoces, qui continuent de compromettre l'avenir de nombreuses jeunes filles dans la région. L’article Koudougou : Une mineure de 16 ans sauvée d’un mariage forcé grâce à une mobilisation citoyenne est apparu en premier sur NetAfrique.net .

US court sets June 2027 trial for Venezuelan President Maduro
items.description: Venezuelan President Nicolas Maduro and his wife Cilia Flores will go on trial starting on June 1, 2027, a US judge ruled on Wednesday. The couple, who pleaded not guilty to drug trafficking and firearms charges, made their third appearance in a New York court after the forcible seizure by US troops in a military raid in early January. During Wednesday's 15-minute hearing, Judge Alvin Hellerstein set the trial date at the request of lawyers on both sides. Maduro's lawyer, Barry Pollack, said he will first challenge the indictment on sovereign immunity grounds because if that is successful, Maduro will not have to continue litigating the case. On January 3, US military forces launched a large-scale strike against Venezuela and forcibly seized Maduro and his wife before transporting them to New York. The US attacks have shocked the international community, triggering a stream of condemnation and serious concerns worldwide. During his first arraignment on January 5, Maduro pleaded not guilty to all US charges against him, including drug trafficking. The couple has been jailed at the Metropolitan Detention Center in Brooklyn, New York, since their seizure in Caracas.

items.title: « L’Afrique retombe en esclavage » : le cri d’alerte visionnaire du président Maurice Yaméogo
items.description: Trente-six (36) ans après une interview accordée à l'hebdomadaire ivoirien Ivoire Dimanche (ID), les propos de Maurice Yaméogo, premier président de la Haute-Volta (actuel Burkina Faso), retrouvent une résonance particulière. Prononcées en 1990, ses analyses dépassent le contexte de l'époque pour poser une question fondamentale : celle de la véritable souveraineté de l'Afrique. À l'heure où de nombreux États africains revendiquent une plus grande maîtrise de leur destin politique, économique et sécuritaire, cette réflexion apparaît d'une étonnante actualité. Au cours de cet entretien, l'ancien chef de l'État ne cache ni les blessures de son parcours politique ni son attachement à son pays. Renversé le 3 janvier 1966, emprisonné durant plusieurs années, puis privé de sa citoyenneté, Maurice Yaméogo affirme avoir accepté la perte du pouvoir avec sérénité. Ce qui l'a le plus meurtri, confie-t-il, ce n'est pas la fin de son mandat, mais le fait d'être devenu « un étranger dans son propre pays », après avoir choisi de céder le pouvoir afin d'éviter une effusion de sang. Mais c'est surtout sa lecture de l'avenir de l'Afrique qui retient l'attention. Avec une lucidité remarquable, il lance cette phrase devenue emblématique : « L'Afrique est en train de retomber en esclavage. » Une affirmation qui ne renvoie pas à l'esclavage d'hier, mais à une dépendance nouvelle, moins visible, fondée sur les rapports économiques, financiers et géostratégiques. Pour Maurice Yaméogo, les indépendances obtenues dans les années 1960 restaient inachevées. Selon lui, l'Afrique a certes accédé à l'indépendance politique, mais elle demeure freinée dans sa marche vers l'émancipation économique. « On nous a octroyé l'indépendance politique et on nous attendait au tournant économique », déclare-t-il, dénonçant les obstacles dressés contre les pays africains qui cherchent à construire leur autonomie. Plus de trois décennies après cette interview, cette réflexion continue d'alimenter les débats sur la souveraineté économique du continent. Contrôle des ressources naturelles, industrialisation, transformation locale des matières premières, diversification des partenariats internationaux, maîtrise des politiques monétaires et financières : autant de sujets qui occupent aujourd'hui une place centrale dans les stratégies de développement de nombreux pays africains. Le Burkina Faso n'échappe pas à cette dynamique. Les discours actuels autour de la reconquête de la souveraineté nationale, de la valorisation des richesses nationales, du renforcement des capacités productives et de la recherche de nouveaux équilibres dans les relations internationales rejoignent, sous plusieurs aspects, les préoccupations exprimées par celui qui a perdu le pouvoir à la suite d’un soulèvement populaire le 3 janvier 1966. Autre enseignement majeur de cette interview : l'appel à l'unité. Maurice Yaméogo regrette que les divisions entre Africains affaiblissent davantage le continent. Pour le premier président de la Haute-Volta (actuel Burkina Faso ), les différends doivent céder la place à la solidarité afin de relever ensemble les défis communs. « Au lieu d'étaler nos divisions et nos rancœurs sur la place publique, nous devrions nous serrer les coudes », a-t-il plaidé à l’époque, convaincu que les solutions viendraient d'abord d'une plus grande cohésion entre les États africains. Si les contextes ont profondément évolué depuis 1990, les interrogations demeurent. Comment bâtir une souveraineté durable ? Comment transformer les immenses richesses du continent en prospérité pour les populations ? Comment garantir une indépendance qui ne soit pas seulement politique, mais également économique, technologique et stratégique ? En relisant aujourd'hui cette interview, on découvre bien plus que les confidences d'un ancien chef d'État. On y retrouve la parole d'un acteur des indépendances africaines qui a mis déjà en garde contre les risques d'une autonomie incomplète. Son cri d'alerte, lancé il y a trente-six ans, continue d'interpeller les consciences et rappelle que la liberté d'une nation ne se résume pas à ses symboles, mais se mesure aussi à sa capacité de définir librement son avenir. À bien des égards, le combat mené par la génération des pères des indépendances trouve aujourd'hui un prolongement dans les aspirations contemporaines des peuples africains : faire de la souveraineté non plus un idéal proclamé, mais une réalité vécue. C'est cette continuité historique qui confère toute sa force et toute sa portée au message laissé par Maurice Yaméogo. Bodoin Agapi FOLANÉ L’article « L'Afrique retombe en esclavage » : le cri d'alerte visionnaire du président Maurice Yaméogo est apparu en premier sur NetAfrique.net .

items.title: Fada N’Gourma : Deux agents de sécurité écroués pour contrôle routier irrégulier
items.description: Alors que l’immense majorité des Forces de défense et de sécurité (FDS) se bat avec abnégation pour la libération du territoire burkinabè, certains comportements isolés viennent jeter une ombre sur cet engagement. Selon les révélations de « Kantigui » dans le quotidien Sidwaya, deux agents des forces de sécurité ont été interpellés le 8 juillet dernier à Fada N'Gourma. Accusés d'avoir instauré un poste de contrôle irrégulier pour rançonner les usagers, ils ont été placés sous mandat de dépôt. Cette affaire, qui mêle concussion et abus de confiance, rappelle la détermination de la hiérarchie à ne laisser aucune place à l'impunité dans les rangs. L’héroïsme des forces engagées au front ne doit pas occulter la vigilance nécessaire face aux dérives internes. C’est le message fort envoyé par l’interpellation de deux éléments des forces de sécurité au début du mois de juillet dans l'Est du pays. Les faits se sont déroulés au secteur 9 de Fada N'Gourma. Profitant de leur statut, les deux agents auraient décidé d'organiser, de leur propre chef, une opération de contrôle routier totalement irrégulière. Sans en aviser leur hiérarchie, ils ont investi les lieux pour intercepter les usagers de la route. Mais derrière l'apparence d'une mission de service public se cachait une entreprise de prédation. En l'espace de quelques heures, ils auraient réussi à extorquer plus de 100 000 F CFA aux citoyens de passage. La supercherie n'a pas duré. Informée de ces agissements qui ternissent l'image de l'institution, la justice a rapidement réagi. Selon les sources de Sidwaya, les deux agents ont été interpellés et font l'objet de poursuites pour des chefs d'accusation graves : association de malfaiteurs, concussion et abus de confiance. Actuellement placés sous mandat de dépôt, les deux mis en cause attendent les conclusions de l’enquête judiciaire qui vient d'être ouverte. Cette procédure vise à déterminer si d'autres complicités existent et à faire la lumière sur l'ampleur exacte des prélèvements illégaux effectués. Pour les populations et les observateurs, cette interpellation est perçue comme un signal de salubrité publique. Elle rappelle que le sacrifice des FDS pour la protection des biens et des personnes ne peut être souillé par des pratiques contraires à l'éthique républicaine. L'affaire de Fada N'Gourma souligne ainsi l'importance de la vigilance hiérarchique et du civisme pour débusquer ceux que l'on appelle désormais les « brebis galeuses » du système sécuritaire. L’article Fada N'Gourma : Deux agents de sécurité écroués pour contrôle routier irrégulier est apparu en premier sur NetAfrique.net .

items.title: Oasis overtake The Beatles in all-time UK album chart
items.description: Oasis’s second album (What’s The Story) Morning Glory? has officially overtaken the biggest-selling album by their heroes The Beatles in the list of the UK’s most popular albums of all-time . The Gallagher brothers’ 1995 album, which contains hits like Wonderwall and Don’t Look Back in Anger, is third on the all-time best-sellers list, published by the Official Charts Company. The Beatles’ 1967 psychedelic masterpiece Sgt Pepper’s Lonely Hearts Club Band, which was third on the same list a decade ago , has now dropped to number five. Queen’s 1981 Greatest Hits collection remains the biggest seller overall with 1992’s Abba Gold in second place, while Adele’s 2011 album 21 has moved up to fourth place. John Lennon, Paul McCartney, Ringo Starr and George Harrison reinvented themselves during their Sgt Pepper era The all-time list was released on Wednesday to mark the 70th anniversary of the weekly UK album chart. In total, (What’s The Story) Morning Glory? has racked up 6.2 million UK chart units – streams and sales combined – according to the Official Charts Company. Last year, there was renewed interest in Oasis’s music due to their much publicised worldwide reunion tour . The setlist lent heavily on tracks from the album, including Some Might Say and Champagne Supernova. In 2022, Queen’s compilation LP made history as the first album to pass seven million UK chart units. Only Swedish pop maestros Abba have breached the seven million mark since, making it an elite club. Meanwhile, Fleetwood Mac’s 1977 classic Rumours and Bob Marley’s best-of album Legend have both moved into the all-time top 10 over the past 10 years. UK Official Albums Chart – all-time top 10 Greatest Hits – Queen Gold – Abba (What’s the Story) Morning Glory – Oasis 21 – Adele Sgt Pepper’s Lonely Hearts Club Band – The Beatles Rumours – Fleetwood Mac The Dark Side of the Moon – Pink Floyd Thriller – Michael Jackson Legend – Bob Marley and The Wailers Brothers in Arms – Dire Straits (What’s the Story) Morning Glory? received surprisingly mixed reviews from the British music press when it was released in October 1995. But it went on to win best British album at the 1996 Brit Awards and has stood the test of time. Wonderwall, from the album, became hugely popular again this summer with England fans and players singing it together at the World Cup in North America. The booming rock ballad Don’t Look Back in Anger also took on a greater significance after it was sung, impromptu, at a vigil for those who lost their lives in the 2017 Manchester Arena attack. Becca Monahan and Chris Austin, interim co-managing directors of the Official Charts, congratulated Oasis on the album’s achievements in a joint statement. “Three decades on, its scale and influence remain extraordinary – a defining British record that continues to unite generations, fill stadiums and soundtrack lives,” they noted. Oasis Songwriter Noel Gallagher was born three days after The Beatles released their Sgt Pepper album. Olivia Rodrigo tops 2020s chart Sour bagged best pop vocal album at the 2022 Grammy Awards The chart – released every Friday in the UK – began on 22 July 1956, with Frank Sinatra’s Songs for Swingin’ Lovers! becoming the first number one. Reflecting more recent tastes, the Charts Company has also published a list of the most-streamed albums of the 2020s, led by Olivia Rodrigo’s Sour. The US singer’s breakout record, which carries hits like Drivers License and Good 4 U, has spent a total of 268 weeks on the chart since its release in June 2021. The critically-acclaimed Sour was inspired by heartbreak and feelings of loneliness. “Nobody understood how I was feeling or related to it, so I think by writing songs about exactly how I felt I was creating like a friend for me,” the pop-rock star said in her 2022 Disney+ documentary Driving Home 2 U. Recent Glastonbury headliner Rodrigo pipped Ed Sheeran’s = and Taylor Swift’s Folklore to the top spot. Swift has two albums in the top 10, which also includes records by Sabrina Carpenter, Noah Kahan, Dua Lipa, Harry Styles, SZA and late US rapper Pop Smoke. The UK’s most-streamed albums of the 2020s so far: Sour – Olivia Rodrigo = – Ed Sheeran Folklore – Taylor Swift Short N’ Sweet – Sabrina Carpenter Midnights – Taylor Swift Stick Season – Noah Kahan Future Nostalgia – Dua Lipa Harry’s House – Harry Styles SOS – SZA Shoot for the Stars Aim for the Moon – Pop Smoke

items.title: Faible taux de réussite au baccalauréat 2025-2026 avec seulement 13,56% d’admis à la première session
items.description: Le taux de réussite à la session ordinaire de l'examen du baccalauréat en Mauritanie pour l'année scolaire 2025-2026 s'est élevé à environ 13,56 %, a annoncé mercredi la ministre de l'Éducation et de la Réforme du système éducatif, Houda Babah, lors d'une conférence de presse consacrée à la proclamation des résultats de l'examen et à l'évaluation du déroulement de la session. La ministre a indiqué que le nombre de candidats à l’examen s’élevait à 64 000, dont 60 376 se sont présentés, contre 49 819 l’année dernière, soit une augmentation de 10 557 candidats. Le nombre de candidats libres s’est quant à lui élevé à 13 905, soit environ 23 % du nombre total de participants. Mme Babah a ajouté que les examens nationaux avaient connu cette année une amélioration sur le plan de l’organisation, ainsi qu’un renforcement des principes de mérite, de transparence et de rigueur à toutes leurs étapes. Elle a souligné que le succès du système éducatif ne se mesure pas uniquement à l’augmentation des taux de réussite, mais aussi à l’ancrage d’une culture du mérite et à la formation de générations dotées des connaissances et des compétences nécessaires pour contribuer au développement. La ministre a précisé que le taux de réussite des élèves issus de milieux défavorisés s’élevait à 64,3 %, tandis que 7,1 % d’entre eux ont été reportés à la deuxième session, expliquant que cette catégorie a bénéficié des programmes des « écoles d’excellence » relevant du projet « L’École républicaine », ainsi que de cours de rattrapage et de bourses d’études. Elle a souligné que ces programmes visent à soutenir les enfants issus de familles en situation de précarité et à leur offrir de meilleures opportunités éducatives, précisant que le ministère continue d’œuvrer à l’amélioration de la qualité de l’enseignement et à la promotion du principe d’égalité des chances entre les différents élèves.

items.title: Nouvelle Loi sur les Libertés religieuses au Burkina Faso: Voici ce qui change pour les signes religieux dans les documents d’identité
items.description: Le paysage juridique burkinabè franchit une étape importante dans la gestion du fait religieux. Par le biais de la loi N°020-2026/ALP du 20 juin 2026, le Ministère de l’Administration Territoriale et de la Mobilité définit désormais les garanties, principes et obligations liés aux libertés de culte. Si le texte consacre le droit de chacun à manifester ses convictions religieuses, y compris par le port de signes visibles, il introduit des limites strictes liées à l'identification des citoyens et au respect de l'ordre public. Un équilibre subtil entre respect des croyances et impératifs de sécurité nationale. Le Burkina Faso se dote d'un nouveau cadre légal pour régir l'expression des convictions de ses citoyens. La loi N°020-2026/ALP, adoptée le 20 juin dernier, vient préciser les modalités d'exercice des libertés religieuses, un sujet au cœur de la cohésion sociale dans le pays. L’article 5 de cette loi réaffirme que toute personne, agissant seule ou en association, dispose de la liberté de manifester sa religion ou ses convictions. Cette manifestation peut être privée ou publique et s’exprime par divers canaux : le culte, l’enseignement, les rites, les pratiques sociales ou encore le port de signes religieux visibles. Toutefois, cette liberté n'est pas absolue. Le législateur a pris soin d'assortir ce droit d'une « clause de réserve ». L'expression de la foi doit impérativement respecter les libertés fondamentales d'autrui, l'ordre public, les bonnes mœurs et, surtout, la dignité de la personne humaine. L'une des innovations majeures de ce texte réside dans l'encadrement des signes religieux sur les documents officiels. L’article 6 stipule qu'il est désormais possible de manifester sa liberté religieuse à travers le port de signes visibles sur les documents administratifs d'identification. Cependant, cette ouverture est strictement encadrée par les nécessités de contrôle de l’État. La loi précise sans équivoque que le port de ces signes est interdit dès lors qu'il constitue une entrave à la vérification de l'identité de la personne. En d'autres termes, la reconnaissance faciale et l'identification sécurisée priment sur l'affichage des convictions religieuses lorsque la clarté de l'identité est en jeu. À travers cette mise au point, le Ministère de l’Administration Territoriale et de la Mobilité semble vouloir prévenir d'éventuels contentieux liés à l'usage des signes religieux dans l'espace public et administratif. En codifiant ce qui relève de la liberté et ce qui relève de l'obligation républicaine, le Burkina Faso tente de concilier la diversité des croyances avec la rigueur nécessaire à la gestion de l'État et de la sécurité collective. L’article Nouvelle Loi sur les Libertés religieuses au Burkina Faso: Voici ce qui change pour les signes religieux dans les documents d’identité est apparu en premier sur NetAfrique.net .

items.title: Secteur de la santé: plus de 90% des agents satisfaits des outils numériques
items.description: L’association Recherche pour la santé et le développement (RESADE) a organisé un atelier de restitution des résultats de l’évaluation de l’écosystème digital minimal, le mercredi 22 juillet 2026, à Ouagadougou. L’association Recherche pour la santé et le développement (RESADE) a commandité une étude sur l’évaluation de l’Ecosystème digital minimal (EDM). Les résultats de l’évaluation ont été restitués le mercredi 22 juillet 2026, à Ouagadougou. Le directeur exécutif de RESADE, Yamba Kafando a expliqué que l’EDM déployé en juillet 2023 dans les districts sanitaires de Ziniaré, Ténado, Manga et Sapouy regroupait huit applications numériques destinées à améliorer la gestion des soins, de la gratuité des prestations des médicaments, des finances et des consultations des femmes enceintes ainsi que des enfants de moins de 5 ans. Il a remercié le ministère de la Santé pour la confiance accordée au RESADE ainsi que les partenaires techniques et financiers et les acteurs des districts concernés pour leur appui et collaboration. Il a réaffirmé la volonté de RESADE dans la production des connaissances pour un système de santé plus performant et résilient. Quant à l’investigateur principal Rémi Kaboré, il a souligné que durant trois ans, l’association RESADE a conduit cinq enquêtes dans 72 centres de santé dont les résultats témoignent d’une forte adhésion des utilisateurs. « Plus de 90% des agents de santé interrogés se déclarent satisfaits ou très satisfaits des outils numériques tandis que 95% identifient l’entraide entre collègues comme un facteur clé d’appropriation », a-t-il indiqué. Selon lui, le taux de médian a atteint 64,3% et neuf centres sur dix affirment être ouverts au changement. « Plus de 75% des agents dénoncent la double saisie des informations sur papier et sur tablette », a-t-il ajouté. Il a par ailleurs relevé que près de 80% des centres de santé ne disposent d’aucun budget pour assurer la maintenance ou le renouvellement des équipements numériques et environ 80% financent leur connexion sur leurs propres ressources. Des données jugées fiables En outre, il a montré que les outils ont produit des données jugées fiables mais celles-ci restent insuffisamment exploitées en raison de l’absence du tableau de bord central prévu par le projet et du manque de compétences analytiques chez les responsables. « Sans un instrument opérationnel de synthèse et de pilotage, les données restent stockées plutôt que mobilisées pour améliorer la gestion des ressources, la planification locale ou les politiques de santé », a-t-il regretté. Pour le secrétaire des réformes techniques sur le financement de la santé, Vincent Batiené, cette initiative n’est pas un simple déploiement technique, mais une transformation des habitudes de travail pour bâtir progressivement une culture de la donnée. « La restitution que nous avons vécue aujourd’hui n’est pas une fin en soi », a-t-il précisé. Les évaluateurs ont formulé des recommandations pour une bonne utilisation de ces numériques à savoir le renforcement des infrastructures, la formation sur l’analyse et l’utilisation des données ainsi que la mise en place d’un tableau de bord central afin que les informations deviennent un levier effectif de la gouvernance. Ils ont appelé aussi à formaliser des budgets locaux pour garantir la continuité des services numériques sans dépendre exclusivement de partenaires externes. Les conclusions de l’évaluation ont eu des retombés concrètes avec l’extension de l’EDM dans la région du centre et la mise en place de l’application E-Flux financier qui a amorcé une montée de prise en charge au niveau national. Abdoulaye BALBONE Assami TIENDREBEOGO (Stagiaire)

items.title: ‘Fake’ agency: Tinubu’s amoral rule breeds impunity, abuse of power! By Olu Fasan
items.description: There is a shocking paradox, a bewildering moral hypocrisy, at the heart of President Bola Tinubu’s leadership. On the one hand, he is actively prosecuting previous public officers for corrupt practices, claiming to have secured over 7,000 convictions since he came to power three years ago. Yet, on the other hand, he presides over a moral cesspit, where ministers routinely abuse power and act with utter impunity, where some are so powerful they can literally get away with murder, protected by their fealty and sycophancy to the president, where governance is utterly devoid of transparency and accountability, and where conflicts of interest are so blatant, with a total blurring of public and private interests. These malfeasances are the subjects of this intervention, but we must start with the ongoing “fake” agency saga. When the story broke recently, my first reaction was: where else in the world would such a thing happen? Where else would a “phantom” agency walk its way through the machinery of government and even penetrate diplomatic fortresses and yet have no legal basis for existing? Where else, except in mafia-run countries? Such a thing can only happen in a country that has reached a moral nadir, where the fish rots from the head down, where the ethos of public service and the norms of propriety in public office are totally non-existent. Think about it. An agency called the Presidential Foreign Intervention Promotion Council, PFIPC, sprung up from nowhere, with one Prince Adeniyi Adeyemi as its director-general. The agency was invested with the whole panoply of a federal parastatal: it had an office at the Federal Secretariat; had civil servants working for it, with approval to hire more than 300 staff; opened accounts with the CBN; and had N1.3bn allocation in the 2026 Appropriation Act. But that’s not all. The agency’s DG had access to and interacted freely with the secretary to the government of the federation, SGF, the accountant-general of the federation, the head of the civil service and the budget office, among others. He also held meetings with foreign diplomats. Then, one day, the government said it knew absolutely nothing about the agency; it was “bogus” and its DG “fake”! President Tinubu was “outraged”and ordered the Independent Corrupt Practices and Other Related Offences Commission, ICPC, to probe the agency’s existence and unmask those behind it. But I repeat: where else would such a thing happen: an entire arm of government emerging and operating freely with no one admitting to facilitating its emergence and operations? In the wholly unlikely event that a “fake” agency like the PFIPC emerged in the US or in Britain, would those countries’ leaders ask a state organ like the ICPC, controlled by the attorney-general, to probe such a matter in which top officials might be implicated? No, in Britain, there would be an independent judicial inquiry; in America, there would be a powerful and wholly independent Congressional hearing. Any public investigation of such a high-level malfeasance must be transparent and above board. But sadly, not in Nigeria! For instance, while President Tinubu ordered the ICPC probe, he pre-empted its outcome by declaring his “100 per cent confidence” in Femi Gbajabiamila, his chief of staff, who allegedly signed the letter of appointment for the agency’s DG. Adeyemi said he paid a N400mn bribe to secure the appointment, but Gbajabiamila denied culpability and sued for damages. Yet, Nigerians deserve a genuinely open and transparent public hearing. But, earlier this week, when the ICPC started its investigation, Gbajabiamila’s lawyer, Jiti Ogunye, told journalists that his client was “grilled” by the ICPC and went back to his “duty post”in the Villa. But who witnessed the “grilling”? Would such “grilling” take place behind closed doors in America, Britain or, indeed, South Africa? Of course not. In those countries, transparency and accountability are hallmarks of governance, and no public officer is untouchable. But President Tinubu has no time for such moral niceties. He will protect any minister, aide or official who is useful to him however putrid the allegations of impropriety swirling around them. It is inconceivable, for instance, that a British minister would be allowed to treat so lightly the mysterious death of someone in his home as David Umahi, Tinubu’s minister of works, appears to be handling the recent death of a nurse, Mary Habila, in his country home in Ebonyi. This is not a question of guilt or innocence, but there are standards of public life that no public officer should breach with impunity. However, abuse of power and impunity are thoroughly embedded in governance in Nigeria, with powerful ministers and officials routinely shielded from accountability. So, truth be told, no one should expect the ICPC to name any politically influential official as a collaborator in the “fake” agency case, even though, as Babachir Lawal, a former SGF, told the BBC: “There must be connivance with officials within,” adding: “You must have officials within the system who will validate such corrupt behaviour.” But who are the officials involved in the PFIPC case? Well, all eyes are on the ICPC to tell Nigerians and, indeed, the world how the PFIPC came to be, how it enjoyed the paraphernalia of a full-fledged federal agency, and how its DG had such wide-ranging access and contacts within government and in the diplomatic community. Which brings us to another pervasive malfeasance: conflicts of interest. In Nigeria, especially under the Tinubu administration, the lines between public and private interests are blurred, with public officers profiting from power. President Tinubu emblemises this phenomenon with the way he allows the perception of conflicts of interest to become entrenched under his rule, the way he treats the Nigerian state as a personal fiefdom by emblazoning his name on major public monuments, at least eight within just three years in power, the latest being the Lagos-Calabar coastal highway. Last week, David Umahi, the beleaguered minister of works, announced that his ministry had decided to rename the coastal highway after Tinubu, calling it “President Bola Ahmed Tinubu Coastal Highway”. But why? Well, Umahi said Tinubu first conceived the project about 27 years ago when he was Lagos State governor. “This is one man who dreams and has the grace and divine mandate to actualise that dream,” he gushed. What a fawning sycophancy! Note that present and future generations of Nigerians are lumbered with the N15trillion debt incurred, so far, to fund the construction of the coastal highway; note, too, that the contract was directly awarded to a company owned by Tinubu’s long-standing business partner, a company on which board his son reportedly sits; now, the same coastal highway is named after Tinubu himself. If there is any other definition of conflicts of interest, I have not seen it yet; except that, here, the conflicts of interest are utterly blatant and outrageously daring. In genuine liberal democracies, such blurring of public and private interests, such conflation of public good with private gain, is frontally tackled and prevented through powerful transparency and accountability mechanisms, through robust checks and balances and through the force of civic norms. Sadly, such guardrails do not exist in Nigeria. As a result, President Tinubu runs a very opaque government that is not accountable for its actions. Recently, the IMF said that public spending worth about 2 per cent of GDP, or N8.8trillion, was not recorded in government budgets, raising concerns about budget transparency and accountability. In a recent story titled “IMF joins backlash against ‘opaque’ loans with crackdown on Nigeria”, the Financial Times said the IMF questioned Nigeria’s use of opaque borrowing. Lack of transparency and accountability is a major problem under Tinubu’s rule. But every leader is governed by his values. In truth, President Tinubu is utterly amoral. He puts efficiency above ethics. But such indifference to issues of propriety is a fertile terrain, a breeding ground, for abuse of power and other malfeasances like the “fake” agency scandal! *Dr Fasan is the author of ‘In The National Interest: The Road to Nigeria’s Political, Economic and Social Transformation’, available at RovingHeights bookstores. The post ‘Fake’ agency: Tinubu’s amoral rule breeds impunity, abuse of power! By Olu Fasan appeared first on Vanguard News .

items.title: Le ministre des mines : les permis d’exploitation minière sont destinés à l’exploitation, et pas à la spéculation et le monopole
items.description: Le ministre des Mines et de l'Industrie, Eddi Ould Zeine, a déclaré que le gouvernement s'efforçait de renforcer la gouvernance dans le secteur minier, soulignant que les concessions minières étaient destinées à l'exploitation des ressources nationales et à la création de valeur ajoutée, et ne constituaient pas un domaine propice à la spéculation ou au monopole. Lors d’une conférence de presse tenue à l’issue du Conseil des ministres, ould Zeine a précisé que les récentes décisions relatives à l’annulation d’un permis de prospection et à l’octroi d’un nouveau permis d’exploitation s’inscrivent dans le cadre de l’application des lois régissant le secteur, dans le but de garantir une utilisation optimale des richesses minières et d’encourager les investisseurs sérieux. Le ministre a ajouté que le ministère s’efforce de réglementer le secteur et de le rendre plus attractif pour les investissements, soulignant que la Mauritanie s’emploie à renforcer sa position sur le marché des minerais, notamment face à la demande mondiale croissante en métaux stratégiques et en éléments de minerais rares. Le ministre a souligné que l’accord-cadre signé avec les États-Unis concernant la coopération dans le domaine des minerais vitaux constitue une avancée pour renforcer les domaines de l’exploration, du traitement et de la valorisation, ce qui permettra d’accroître la valeur ajoutée des ressources minérales au niveau local. Sur le plan industriel, le ministre a déclaré que la campagne de mise en conformité des usines et des installations industrielles visait à aligner les entreprises sur la nouvelle législation, à améliorer la qualité de la production et à renforcer la compétitivité de l’industrie nationale. Il a souligné que le ministère s’efforçait parallèlement de simplifier les démarches pour les investisseurs grâce à la mise en place d’un guichet unique destiné à accélérer l’octroi des autorisations et la régularisation des situations juridiques.

items.title: The Smithsonian’s Show Trial on Capitol Hill
items.description: The nation’s most scrutinized museum director showed up to Congress this week acutely aware of her audience. Anthea Hartig, the head of the Smithsonian’s National Museum of American History, referred to the institution as “your” museum. She emphatically affirmed her love of this country. She even cited her family’s Revolutionary War roots. Hartig had been called to testify at two hearings that House Republicans had organized in response to a 162-page White House report accusing the American-history museum of “extreme political activism.” But her opening remarks, in which she also asserted that the report “does not fairly characterize the full body of work of this museum,” didn’t ward off a patriotism sniff test. “Do you love America?” Representative Eric Burlison of Missouri, a Republican, asked Hartig yesterday during a hearing held by the House Delivering on Government Efficiency Subcommittee. “I love this country unconditionally,” Hartig replied, a sentiment that she would repeat at least half a dozen times during this de facto trial on Capitol Hill. Whether she confirmed or denied her allegiances didn’t actually matter. Hartig mounted a defense that the museum is “committed to rigorous, fact-based, museum-quality experiences” and acknowledges “enduring debates so that visitors can understand how we’ve wrestled with democracy and citizenship and liberty and national identity.” She called telling the full story of American history “a form of respect for the American people.” But the GOP’s judgment had clearly already been cast. The hearings, rather than serving as opportunities for information gathering, were meant only to widen Donald Trump’s attack on the Smithsonian by enlisting Congress in his efforts to reshape public memory. Hartig’s appearance followed more than a year of pressure on the Smithsonian from the White House as it has sought to cleanse “improper ideology” from the institution and combat what it sees as a pessimistic approach to history that is too focused on—in Trump’s words—“how bad slavery was.” They marked the most public and outwardly combative test of the institution yet, as some members outright scolded Hartig. Trump’s interest in the Smithsonian has already resulted in changes to wall text discussing his impeachments, and in National Portrait Gallery Director Kim Sajet’s resignation after Trump tried to fire her on social media last year. But the White House has not attempted to overhaul the Smithsonian like it has the Kennedy Center, and it can’t—the institution’s unique legal status and leadership structure insulate it from the president’s whims. By contrast, Congress appropriates much of the public-private institution’s funding and exercises oversight over the institution, which is governed by a board of regents. [ Read: How the Smithsonian could fall ] Over the course of two days of congressional testimony, Democrats challenged the framing of the museum as politically biased and defended the Smithsonian’s independence, including with their own report , noting errors in the White House document. Republicans, meanwhile, echoed the administration’s claims and dissected Hartig’s public statements, particularly an offhand comment made during a 2021 webinar for the Slave Dwelling Project Conference in which she said, “As we all know in this conference, loving America is very complicated.” The White House had plucked the quote for its report as an example of what it sees as Hartig’s lack of patriotism, and Trump allies in Congress regurgitated it this week like it was her personal slogan, during hearings that quickly took the tenor of a loyalty test. Perhaps, in a way, it was: The Yale historian David Blight, who also testified both days, noted that in its July 4 report claiming that the museum had strayed from its mission, the Trump administration cited statements dating from the institution’s opening without considering the political context that might have sparked such “triumphalism”: the Cold War. The Heritage Foundation fellow Mike Gonzalez, whom Republicans called as a witness both days, was playing his role in Tuesday’s time warp of a hearing when he leveled accusations of Marxism at the museum before his call to “clean out house” at the Smithsonian. (Witnesses also included the Hillsdale College professor Matthew Spalding yesterday and the president of the conservative think tank American Main Street Initiative, Jeffrey H. Anderson, today.) Whether “love” is really the metric by which one should assess a historian’s credibility seemed outside the scope of the discussion. Nuance was treated like a threat. It seemed to be no coincidence that the two points that GOP members took issue with most were Hartig’s statement about the complexities of patriotic love and an exhibit that had closed more than three years ago, called “Girlhood (It’s Complicated),” which touched on the struggles of transgender youth. This allergy to complexity in the representatives’ approach to questions was unmissable. There was a recurring pattern: A GOP member would ask an extremely loaded question—whether the museum should display materials with sexual themes, for instance—and Hartig would try, seemingly in earnest, to respond, and almost instantly she’d be met with: Yes or no! It’s a yes-or-no question! In one particularly illustrative moment today, Representative Mike Carey asked Hartig what she meant when she said she wanted to change the museum’s “America First” approach. She had barely uttered the words “I would argue that the American-history museum” before he cut her off, asserting that she wasn’t answering the question. Serving in Congress and running a history museum seemed like they might be professions that are too alien to each other to interact. GOP lawmakers repeatedly employed the kind of narrow logic and treacly sentiments that historians cringe at, such as when Representative Pat Fallon asked Hartig to agree that America “is the greatest nation history has ever known.” Burlison suggested to Hartig that, with the World Cup bringing legions of foreign tourists to the country, “you would think” that the Smithsonian would be “the one institution that would be trumpeting the great and wonderful things that this country has done.” Again and again, Hartig’s GOP interlocutors seemed to be operating from a definition of a museum that sees its role as dispensing propaganda. Anyone who had come to the Hill hoping to gain insights into the American-history museum’s actual philosophy or operations would have been hard-pressed to find them. The first hearing began with a YouTube-style mashup of Hartig giving land acknowledgements. The second, held by the House Administration Committee, ended with a staffer trotting out, for at least the third time during the two days, a poster board emblazoned with photos of drag queens and other exhibition materials. Critiques of the museums fell into a few categories. One line of criticism involved selecting artifacts that lawmakers disliked and seeking to cast them as representative of the 1.7 million–object history museum. Another offered a kind of museum-edition whataboutism, in which representatives played curator and gave exhibit suggestions: “Did you have a new one for George Washington for the 250th?” one member asked. “How ’bout Thomas Jefferson?” This seemed to misunderstand the limitations of space and time in a museum that has no shortage of Founding Fathers material. Another lawmaker on Wednesday opined about the historic importance of banjos (the museum has 45) and how the creatures that Lewis and Clark discovered should be discussed in exhibits (which would seem to be the purview of the natural-history museum next door). A related line of attack took issue with the tone of the museum but again felt strained. The National Museum of American History is unpatriotic and doesn’t honor the country’s founding enough, members suggested, never mind the huge room dedicated to the star-spangled banner or the buff George Washington statue. Hartig seemed to have shown up prepared for this argument on Day 2: “We have a dozen exhibitions that include the founding era right now: 368 objects, 340 labels. George Washington is mentioned 54 times. You have to walk by a whole section on George Washington in the Kenneth Behring Hall of Military History to get to a 15-foot-tall reproduction of the Declaration of Independence,” she said. GOP representatives also repeatedly stressed the idea that America had led the world in ending slavery and that the museum should highlight that. Blight, the Yale professor, corrected the record: What is unique about the United States is not that it abolished slavery, because many nations did before it , but that it took killing hundreds of thousands of citizens in a civil war to do so. Over the years, the Smithsonian has operated outside the influence of the White House, and much of its work has been celebrated on both sides of the aisle—look no further than President George W. Bush’s efforts to establish the National Museum of African American History and Culture and praise for its nuanced telling of difficult history, which Representative Jonathan Jackson of Illinois, a Democrat, applauded yesterday. Still, the Smithsonian has never been impervious to political headwinds either. This week’s grillings recalled a controversy from the 1990s, when a planned Air and Space Museum exhibit about the Enola Gay had Congress threatening to eradicate its funding from the institution wholesale. The debate about whether the airplane that had dropped an atomic bomb over Hiroshima should be displayed alongside evidence of the devastation that it had caused had the American Legion and the Air Force Association coming after museum leadership with similar allegations of subpar patriotism. The Smithsonian secretary at the time, Michael Heyman, defended his colleagues on ABC’s Nightline , using words that could be coming out of current Smithsonian Secretary Lonnie Bunch’s mouth: “When one is dealing with momentous events, showing a full picture of their occurrence so long as it’s unbiased and it’s balanced is a role for the Smithsonian,” Heyman said. That defense did not hold, however. The Air and Space Museum Director Martin Harwit, who had pushed for the full story of the Enola Gay, resigned. The exhibit that eventually opened had minimal text and none of the historical context that he had intended. During that 1994 Nightline segment, the interviewer Aaron Brown offered a question to Heyman and American Legion National Commander William Detweiler that feels eerily applicable today. Maybe, Brown said, “what the Legion wants here is a memorial, a commemoration—and what the Smithsonian wanted was something else, was a history.”

items.title: After Orire rescue, what next?
items.description: By SUNNY IKHIOYA The Orire school kidnapping ended on a happy note for the whole country, although a sad one for the family of the slaughtered maths teacher, Oyedokun. Overall, we give thanks; it could have been worse. For once, in a long time, the terrorists’ campaign ended in a total loss for them, with no concession granted to any of their requests. For once, the intelligence capabilities of the Nigerian combined armed forces were put to work with absolute success. For once, the professionalism of our soldiers was put to the test and they excelled. So, all along, it has always been a lack of willpower on the part of the people running our country. Now that the weakness of the bandits has been laid bare under this dispensation, it is time to put an end to their activities all over the country. By this single operation, the Nigerian armed forces have restored the dignity and respect that they once enjoyed in the seventies to the nineties. That they are a well-trained and professional force is no longer in doubt; now is the time to put the activities of those clowns to permanent rest. They must adhere strictly to the instructions of the minister of defence, to take the battle to the bandits and attack them without waiting for orders. They must now be proactive instead of being reactive as they have been in the past. The saboteurs among them must be identified and neutralised. The solution to banditry and terrorism has never been through negotiations; it is always by strong will and superior power, backed by strong passion for the national cause. We must begin to evaluate our orientation and culture towards our oneness. Ideologies, religions and cultures that are antithetical to our common goal and unity must be brutally suppressed. We must return to the Nigeria where citizens are free to travel anywhere they want, anytime they want, without fear of molestation, harassment, kidnapping and killing. Yes, it is possible because this was the Nigeria we grew up in during the days of yore, the sixties to the nineties. Solve the problem of insecurity and religious fanaticism and the glory of Nigeria will begin to shine again. It was predicted that this country would be no more by 2015, but somehow our amoebic—shapeless and formless—nature has enabled us to wobble and stumble on. Some agents of destabilisation had sown seeds of discord among us using religion and ethnicity. They were bent on bringing the nation to its knees, went as far as importing foreigners masquerading as religious and ethnic brothers; it was a total catastrophe. The northern cities and towns lie desolate, living in constant fear and subjugation to bandit interests. Foreign business buccaneers come into our territory, do whatever they like with our citizens and cart away our precious resources without any scruples. It was and is still very bad in the north, and they have been emboldened to extend their campaigns to the south of the country, and that is where we are now. It is important that we consolidate on the Orire gain by taking the battle to them now. This will also help quench the rumour that the release of the teachers and students was an arranged one. No matter the rumours that are being churned out, one thing is clear: the intelligence gathering and implementation was spot on, which destabilised the plans of the ISWAP terrorists. We must go after them before they commit further damage to the national foundation. As they were releasing the Orire victims on Tuesday, July 14, armed men stormed the Government Secondary School, Olowa, Dekina local government area of Kogi State and abducted the principal and students who were writing the National Examination Council, NECO, examination. According to the Vanguard newspaper of July 19, “The incident sparked fears of yet another prolonged school kidnapping. However, before those fears could deepen, Kogi State government announced on Thursday that all victims had been rescued safely and reunited with security agencies for debriefing and medical evaluation.” Although the response is heartwarming, the same Vanguard headlined a new story thus: “After Orire, Kogi operation: 529 kidnapped school children, worshippers, farmers, others remain in captivity.” The government must find all means to release these people and others still in captivity. The bandits, kidnappers and terrorists must be put under constant retreat; they should not be allowed to reassemble. Wherever they are, find them using local security and intelligence, then bomb them out. We will not succeed if we do not go after religious leaders preaching hate and divisiveness in the land, whether in the mosques or churches. There are too many loose cannons flying over our lands, with no control whatsoever. The aides to religious organisations must be monitored and also, foreign scholarships and trainings should be scrutinised. I read about the country of Burkina Faso taking a roll call of its students undergoing studies in Saudi Arabia, where 800 of them are reading religious studies. The leadership questioned how this could bring development to their country. The same thing is happening in our country, where students go abroad to study and end up getting radicalised and return as dissidents against their own country. We must discourage radical preaching and teachings in our lands; the government must not take this for granted. Find the sponsors of these insurgents and deal with them decisively. There must be a new orientation for the people on the values of our unity. Make education compulsory for northern children, return victims of terrorism to their homes and properly rehabilitate them. This is so that hatred will not continue into generations yet unborn. Let the healing be total. In the light of the above, we must look at wider governance. Make governance transparent. Stories like that of Adeyemi Adeniyi breed discontent in the land. Give everyone a sense of belonging; there must be no discrimination in any hue or form. People must be able to live freely and seek for elections anywhere they live without discrimination. This is because the source of discontent in man is injustice. When there is fairness and equitable distribution of resources, done in a transparent manner, the people are happy. Even this war against terrorism is not being done in a transparent manner; there are too many saboteurs both within and outside government. President Jonathan once admitted this fact while he was in government. President Tinubu must learn from this. •Ikhioya writes from Lagos. The post After Orire rescue, what next? appeared first on Vanguard News .

items.title: Why WhatsApp usernames could upset Somalia’s anti-terror fight
items.description: Somalia’s government has told the BBC it is concerned about WhatsApp’s plan to launch a new feature that would let people chat using usernames rather than phone numbers, saying it could pose a security risk. Communications and Technology Minister Ahmed Osman Dirie said it could make it harder to trace and counter criminals in a country that is still battling Islamist militants. The rollout to three billion global users of WhatsApp worldwide is expected in the coming months, allowing people to remove or change their usernames at any time. Somalia has been hit by a brutal and deadly insurgency – led by al-Shabab, an al-Qaeda affiliate – for more than two decades. In response to Somalia’s concerns, WhatsApp told the BBC that the feature was not yet live, and users would still need a phone number to use the app. It further stated that the feature comes with built-in safeguards against scams. But Dirie told the BBC Focus on Africa podcast that the Somali government had approached Meta, which owns WhatsApp, about their concerns and hoped that “we’ll reach a resolution on this matter very soon”. He pointed out that Somalia had a “unique context”, with an economy that heavily relies on mobile money transactions. “Unverified and untraceable usernames” introduced the risk of financial fraud and citizens becoming vulnerable to scammers, he said. Al-Shabab militants have also been known to use WhatsApp for their operations, including for extortion and propaganda. In 2024, Somalia’s intelligence agency said it had shut down 20 WhatsApp groups allegedly operated by al-Shabab for extortion and intimidation. It said it had also disabled data services for about 2,500 phone numbers associated with them. Dirie told the BBC that while Somalia effectively regulated phone numbers in the country, WhatsApp’s new feature would undermine that. Somalia’s issues follow a similar complaint raised by India – the biggest market for the platform with more than 850 million users. Earlier this month, India said it believed usernames might increase online fraud, impersonation and other crimes by allowing criminals to contact potential victims without disclosing their phone numbers. It also asked the company “not to roll out this feature until the consultation on this point is achieved to the satisfaction of the government”. Dirie said linking a WhatsApp profile to a phone number made it easier to track criminals. “So moving away from phone numbers to usernames, we believe, will make it difficult for us to counter those crimes,” he said. The minister said they required further assurances from Meta, despite its assertion that it had built mechanisms to defend against cybercrimes through WhatsApp. He demanded that Meta demonstrate that hiding phone numbers would not “erode digital traceability” and provide “specific safeguards” against financial fraud. “If they prove that, when we discuss if we put those safeguards in place, then we will not have any issue with rolling out the new feature,” he said. Shabab fighters have been known to use WhatsApp for their operations According to Moses Kemibaro, a technology blogger who focuses on Africa, WhatsApp’s new feature aims to enhance user privacy. He views the change as an evolution of WhatsApp to become like other services under Meta, such as Instagram, where one’s identity is not related to a phone number. “It’s almost saying that this can work irrespective of the phone number and just give you a single identity,” he says. While this can enable more private interactions, Kemibaro notes that countries have valid concerns about its impact on security. “[It is] a very sensitive issue around the possibility of escalation in scams and risky propositions that might actually come out of this new trend,” he says. Meta needs to explain the implication of the changes – including whether regulators can link crimes committed by a username to a person behind it, the blogger adds. Kenyan security expert George Musamali partly concurs, saying what WhatsApp is doing helps guarantee a user’s privacy. He says that when people complain about their details being taken from WhatsApp groups and used against them, the blame often falls on the tech giant. There may also be cases when some governments use information shared in such groups to target their critics, which, Musamali argues, might be what Meta is trying to prevent. Ultimately, he suggests that governments may be “jumping the gun”, citing a need for a balance between privacy and security concerns.

items.title: Fast-tracking the National ID project
items.description: Nigeria should press ahead with finishing its national identity project, and fast. No modern state can govern, plan, or protect a population it cannot properly count. After nearly two decades of fits and starts, the country is finally within sight of getting this right. The numbers tell part of the story. More than 137 million Nigerians and legal residents are now enrolled in the National Identity database, up from roughly 117 million barely a year and a half ago. President Tinubu has signed a new law, the NIMC Act 2026, replacing the original 2007 statute, and has ordered the Commission to capture every last citizen by the end of this year. This shows the administration’s resolve to achieve results in this sector, and that is also an ambitious deadline for a country whose actual population is still a matter of estimates rather than fact — anywhere between 200 million and 250 million. A serious identity system will finally largely settle that question. None of this erases the project’s real troubles. Enrolment has been slowed for years by weak infrastructure, patchy funding, sparse centres, and the sheer difficulty of reaching remote communities. Public trust has taken hits too. Many Nigerians believe the database has, at various points, been too centralised, too slow, and too exposed to abuse. This includes persistent allegations that foreign nationals brought into the country during the Buhari years were registered alongside citizens. Whether or not that charge holds up to scrutiny, the fact that it circulates widely and is believed is itself a problem. A national identity system is only as strong as the public’s confidence that no one can quietly slip through its checks. There is also a harder truth about security. Linking NIN to SIM cards and bank accounts was supposed to strip criminals of their anonymity. Yet kidnappers, bandits, and terrorists continue to operate with little visible constraint. This project is both defective and unfinished business. A database that isn’t universal, rigorously verified, and intelligently tied into policing and prosecution cannot, on its own, defeat organised crime. A full register makes fraud and duplicate identities harder to pull off. It lets the government target welfare, health, education, and voting systems with real precision instead of guesswork. Its linkage to bank accounts benefits the people and promotes good government. And it gives the state an honest count of the people it serves. The answer to years of delay is not to abandon the project, nor to rush it recklessly, but to discipline it — tighter verification, tougher border checks, stronger data protection, and unrelenting follow-through until every citizen is captured. Nigeria does not need this system for prestige. It needs it for order, security, and the basic capacity to govern. Let’s finish the job without further delay. The post Fast-tracking the National ID project appeared first on Vanguard News .
